questions.prompt: The orthocenter of a triangle is the intersection of:
choices: Three medians
Three altitudes
Three perpendicular bisectors
Three angle bisectors
hints: Altitudes = perpendiculars from a vertex to the opposite side.
Their meet is the orthocenter.
questions.explanation: Orthocenter = intersection of the three altitudes.

questions.prompt: Where is the orthocenter located in a right triangle?
choices: At the midpoint of the hypotenuse
At the right-angle vertex
At the centroid
Outside the triangle
hints: The two legs of a right triangle are already altitudes.
Their intersection is the right-angle vertex.
questions.explanation: In a right triangle, two of the altitudes are the legs themselves, meeting at the right-angle vertex. So $H$ sits exactly there.

questions.prompt: In which type of triangle is the orthocenter OUTSIDE the triangle?
choices: Acute
Right
Obtuse
Equilateral
hints: For acute: inside. Right: on the right-angle vertex. Obtuse: ?
The obtuse case pushes the orthocenter outside.
questions.explanation: Obtuse triangles: the orthocenter lies *outside* the triangle, near the obtuse-angle vertex.

questions.prompt: What is true for the orthocenter of an equilateral triangle?
choices: It coincides with the centroid, circumcenter, and incenter.
It lies on one specific side.
It is at infinity.
It is on the perimeter.
hints: Equilateral = maximum symmetry.
All four classical centers collapse to one point.
questions.explanation: In an equilateral triangle, the incenter, centroid, circumcenter, and orthocenter all coincide at a single point — the unique 'center' of the equilateral triangle.

questions.promptTex: \text{Right triangle with vertices } A(0,0), B(6,0), C(0,8). \text{ Orthocenter location?}
hints: Right angle at A. Orthocenter sits at the right-angle vertex.
questions.explanation: Right triangles have orthocenter at the right-angle vertex. $A(0,0)$ has the right angle (legs along x and y axes).

questions.prompt: The Euler relation tells us that for ANY (non-equilateral) triangle, the orthocenter H, centroid G, and circumcenter O are:
choices: Always the same point
Collinear, with G dividing OH in ratio 1:2
Vertices of an equilateral triangle
Mutually perpendicular
hints: O, G, H lie on the Euler line.
Ratio is OG:GH = 1:2.
questions.explanation: O, G, H are collinear on the Euler line. G lies between O and H, with OG:GH = 1:2.

questions.promptTex: \text{If } O = (2, 1) \text{ and } G = (4, 3), \text{ find the orthocenter } H \text{ using } H = 3G - 2O.
hints: $H = 3G - 2O$. Plug in coordinates.

questions.prompt: Which property best describes the 'orthocentric system' fact?
choices: All four classical centers are always distinct.
Each of A, B, C, H is the orthocenter of the triangle formed by the other three.
The orthocenter is always the same as the centroid.
The orthocenter is the centroid of the circumcircle.
hints: Take H, B, C — what's the orthocenter of that triangle?
Answer: A. Symmetric in all four points.
questions.explanation: Given a triangle's orthocenter $H$, the four points $A, B, C, H$ form an *orthocentric system*: each is the orthocenter of the triangle formed by the other three. This is a deep symmetry — useful in olympiad geometry.

questions.prompt: In an ACUTE triangle (every angle less than 90°), where does the orthocenter lie?
choices: Outside the triangle, beyond one of the sides
Exactly at a vertex
Inside the triangle
On the perimeter of the triangle
hints: Recall: obtuse → outside, right → at the right-angle vertex. What's left for acute?
Each altitude drops from a vertex INTO the triangle in the acute case.
questions.explanation: Three cases, no gaps: obtuse → outside the triangle, right → at the right-angle vertex, acute → inside. In an acute triangle, every altitude lands inside the triangle, so their meeting point must be inside too.
steps: **Three-way classification.** The orthocenter's position depends on the triangle's largest angle: obtuse → outside, right → at the right-angle vertex, acute → inside.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,30 50,160 230,160" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="140" y1="30" x2="140" y2="160" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><line x1="50" y1="160" x2="195" y2="95" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><line x1="230" y1="160" x2="85" y2="95" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.4" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><circle cx="140" cy="115" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="50" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="230" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="22" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="36" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="236" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="118" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#16a34a">H</text><text x="50" y="200" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">acute triangle — three altitudes meet inside at H</text></svg>

steps: **Apply to acute.** With every angle below 90°, every altitude stays within the triangle. Their concurrence (H) is also inside.

**Pattern lesson.** Slide one angle from acute through 90° to obtuse. The orthocenter starts inside, jumps to the right-angle vertex at exactly 90°, then leaves the triangle entirely. Like the circumcenter, $H$ is a sensitive angle-classifier.

questions.promptTex: \text{Right triangle with legs } 6 \text{ and } 8. \\ \text{Find the length of the altitude from the right-angle vertex to the hypotenuse.}
hints: Compute the area two ways: $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{leg}_1 \cdot \text{leg}_2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{hyp} \cdot h$.
Shortcut: $h = \dfrac{\text{leg}_1 \cdot \text{leg}_2}{\text{hyp}}$.
questions.explanation: Compute the area in two ways. $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2}(6)(8) = 24$. Also $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2}(\text{hyp})(h)$. Hypotenuse $= 10$, so $h = 48/10 = 4.8$.
steps: **Trick — area two ways.** Compute the right triangle's area first using the legs as base/height. Then re-express it using the hypotenuse as the base and $h$ (the altitude from the right-angle vertex) as the height.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="40,160 200,160 40,50" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><rect x="40" y="150" width="10" height="10" fill="none" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.2"/><line x1="40" y1="160" x2="107" y2="114" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><rect x="100" y="117" width="8" height="8" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.1" transform="rotate(-34 104 121)"/><circle cx="40" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="200" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="40" cy="50" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="27" y="178" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="206" y="178" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="27" y="43" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="110" y="178" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">8</text><text x="22" y="108" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">6</text><text x="135" y="108" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">hyp = 10</text><text x="65" y="142" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">h</text></svg>

steps: **Compute area from the legs.** $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 6 \cdot 8 = 24$.
**Find the hypotenuse.** $\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$.
**Solve for $h$.** $24 = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 10 \cdot h \;\Longrightarrow\; h = \dfrac{48}{10} = 4.8$.

**Shortcut to remember.** For a right triangle, $h = \dfrac{\text{leg}_1 \cdot \text{leg}_2}{\text{hyp}}$ — the foot of the altitude divides the hypotenuse into the two geometric-mean pieces (also the formula behind the classical 'geometric mean in a right triangle' result).

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between an ALTITUDE and a PERPENDICULAR BISECTOR of a triangle?
choices: They're the same thing.

An altitude starts from a vertex and is perpendicular to the opposite side; a perpendicular bisector passes through the MIDPOINT of a side and is perpendicular to it — it does not have to involve any vertex.
Altitudes bisect angles; perpendicular bisectors bisect sides.
Altitudes pass through the centroid; perpendicular bisectors pass through the circumcenter.
hints: Each is a 'perpendicular' line, but they pin onto the triangle at different anchors.
Altitudes start at vertices; perpendicular bisectors start at midpoints.
questions.explanation: An ALTITUDE is the perpendicular from a vertex to the line containing the opposite side. A PERPENDICULAR BISECTOR cuts a side at its midpoint, perpendicular to it — independent of the vertices. In equilateral triangles they coincide; in general triangles they're different lines. Altitudes → orthocenter; perpendicular bisectors → circumcenter.
steps: **Altitudes anchor at vertices.** From each vertex, the altitude is the perpendicular dropped to (the line of) the opposite side. The foot is somewhere on that side or its extension.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,40 50,160 230,160" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="140" y1="40" x2="140" y2="160" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><line x1="140" y1="100" x2="140" y2="180" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5 3" opacity="0.0"/><line x1="95" y1="40" x2="180" y2="170" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><circle cx="140" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="140" cy="100" r="3.5" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="140" cy="40" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="50" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="230" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="32" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="36" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="236" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">altitude</text><text x="148" y="170" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">foot</text><text x="190" y="140" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb" font-style="italic">perp. bisector</text></svg>

steps: **Perpendicular bisectors anchor at midpoints.** From the midpoint of each side, draw a line perpendicular to that side. The vertices play no role in the line's position.
**Pattern lesson.** Altitudes → orthocenter (vertices matter). Perpendicular bisectors → circumcenter (midpoints matter). Two different perpendicular constructions, two different centers.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with vertices } A(0,0), B(6,0), C(2,4). \\ \text{Find the orthocenter as } (x_H, y_H), \text{ and report } x_H + y_H.
hints: Altitude from $C$ is perpendicular to $AB$ (the x-axis): vertical line $x = 2$.
Altitude from $A$ is perpendicular to $BC$. $BC$ slope $= (4-0)/(2-6) = -1$, so altitude has slope $+1$: $y = x$.
Intersect: $x = 2, y = 2$. $H = (2, 2)$, so $x_H + y_H = 4$.
questions.explanation: Find any two altitudes and intersect. (1) Altitude from $C(2,4)$ perpendicular to $AB$ (on x-axis) is vertical: $x = 2$. (2) Altitude from $A(0,0)$ perpendicular to $BC$. $BC$ has slope $(4-0)/(2-6) = -1$, so the altitude has slope $+1$ through origin: $y = x$. Intersection: $(2, 2)$. So $H = (2, 2)$ and $x_H + y_H = 4$.
steps: **Recognize the recipe.** Find the orthocenter by intersecting any two altitudes (the third is forced through by concurrence).

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="50,180 200,180 100,80" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="100" y1="80" x2="100" y2="180" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><line x1="50" y1="180" x2="150" y2="130" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><circle cx="100" cy="130" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><circle cx="50" cy="180" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="200" cy="180" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="100" cy="80" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="32" y="196" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A(0,0)</text><text x="206" y="196" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B(6,0)</text><text x="88" y="72" font-size="12" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C(2,4)</text><text x="108" y="133" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#16a34a">H</text><text x="50" y="214" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">altitudes from A and C intersect at H = (2, 2)</text></svg>

steps: **Altitude from $C$.** Side $AB$ lies on the x-axis (slope 0), so the perpendicular from $C(2, 4)$ is the vertical line $x = 2$.
**Altitude from $A$.** Side $BC$ goes from $(6, 0)$ to $(2, 4)$ with slope $\dfrac{4-0}{2-6} = -1$. The altitude from $A$ is perpendicular to $BC$, so its slope is $+1$. Through $A(0,0)$: $y = x$.
**Intersect.** $x = 2$ and $y = x = 2$. So $H = (2, 2)$.

**Verify with altitude from $B$.** Side $AC$ has slope $4/2 = 2$, so the altitude has slope $-1/2$ through $B(6,0)$: $y = -\tfrac{1}{2}(x-6) = -\tfrac{x}{2} + 3$. At $x = 2$: $y = -1 + 3 = 2$ ✓ — passes through $H = (2,2)$.

questions.prompt: A beautiful classical result: where does the REFLECTION of the orthocenter H over any side of the triangle land?
choices: At the centroid
Outside the plane of the triangle
On the CIRCUMCIRCLE of the triangle
Back at H — H is its own reflection
hints: Reflecting H over side BC produces a point H'. It turns out H' lies somewhere special.
It's on the circle through A, B, C.
questions.explanation: Reflecting $H$ over any side of the triangle produces a point that lies on the circumcircle. This is a famous classical fact — used in olympiad geometry and the basis for the *nine-point circle* construction. Equivalently: the foot of each altitude is the midpoint between $H$ and the reflection on the circumcircle.
steps: **Statement.** Reflect $H$ over side $BC$ to get $H'$. Then $H'$ lies on the triangle's circumcircle.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><circle cx="140" cy="110" r="78" fill="none" stroke="#cbd5e1" stroke-width="1.2"/><polygon points="140,32 72,149 208,149" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="140" cy="125" r="4" fill="#16a34a"/><line x1="140" y1="125" x2="140" y2="173" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 3"/><circle cx="140" cy="173" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><line x1="72" y1="149" x2="208" y2="149" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.5"/><circle cx="140" cy="32" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="72" cy="149" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="208" cy="149" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="25" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="58" y="162" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="214" y="162" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="128" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#16a34a">H</text><text x="148" y="178" font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="#dc2626">H'</text><text x="40" y="210" font-size="11" fill="#475569" font-style="italic">reflect H across BC → H' lies on the circumcircle</text></svg>

steps: **Why?** A direct angle-chase: the inscribed-angle theorem + the fact that $\angle BHC = 180° - \angle A$ (well-known) → $\angle BH'C = \angle A$, the inscribed angle on the opposite arc.

**Consequence — nine-point circle.** The foot of the altitude from $A$ is the midpoint of segment $HH'$. The midpoints of $HA$, $HB$, $HC$, the three altitude feet, and the three side-midpoints all lie on a single circle called the *nine-point circle*. Radius: $R/2$. Center: midpoint of $OH$.

questions.promptTex: \text{In a triangle with circumradius } R = 5 \text{ and angle } A = 60°, \\ \text{find } |AH| \text{ (distance from vertex } A \text{ to orthocenter } H\text{).}
hints: Identity: $|AH| = 2R\cos A$.
questions.explanation: A clean classical identity: $|AH| = 2R \cos A$ for each vertex of an acute triangle. Plug in $R = 5$, $\cos 60° = 1/2$: $|AH| = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot (1/2) = 5$. (For an obtuse vertex this is signed — $\cos A$ would be negative — but the distance $|AH| = 2R|\cos A|$ still.)
steps: **Recall the identity.** $|AH| = 2R \cos A$. Mirror identities at $B$ and $C$: $|BH| = 2R \cos B$, $|CH| = 2R \cos C$.

**Why is it true? (Sketch).** Drop the altitude from $A$ to side $a$; its foot is $D$. Then $AD = b \cdot \sin C$ (right triangle $ADC$). Apply the Extended Law of Sines $c = 2R \sin C$, etc. After a few substitutions: $|AH| = 2R \cos A$.
**Sanity check.** When $A = 90°$, $\cos A = 0$, so $|AH| = 0$ → $H = A$. Matches the right-triangle case (orthocenter sits at the right-angle vertex). ✓

questions.prompt: Why do the three altitudes of any triangle CONCUR (meet at a single point)?
choices: Coincidence — works only for special triangles.

Build an auxiliary triangle whose sides are parallel to ABC's, with A, B, C as MIDPOINTS. The altitudes of ABC become the perpendicular bisectors of the auxiliary triangle — which are known to concur.
Because the centroid coincides with the orthocenter.
It's a Euclidean axiom — not provable.
hints: There's a clever construction that turns the altitude problem into a perpendicular-bisector problem.
Draw lines through each vertex parallel to the opposite side. The altitudes of the original become perpendicular bisectors of the bigger triangle.
questions.explanation: Classical proof (due to Euclid via Carnot's construction). Through each vertex, draw a line parallel to the opposite side. The three lines form a larger triangle whose sides have $A, B, C$ as midpoints. The altitudes of $\triangle ABC$ are exactly the *perpendicular bisectors* of this larger triangle. Since perpendicular bisectors of any triangle concur (at its circumcenter), so do the altitudes of $\triangle ABC$.
steps: **Convert one concurrence into another.** Through each vertex, draw a line parallel to the opposite side. The three parallel lines form a larger triangle $A'B'C'$ with $A, B, C$ as midpoints.

**Identify the altitudes inside the bigger triangle.** The altitude of $\triangle ABC$ from $A$ is perpendicular to $BC$. Since the side of $\triangle A'B'C'$ adjacent to $A$ is parallel to $BC$, the same line is perpendicular to that side too — through its midpoint $A$. So the altitude of $\triangle ABC$ from $A$ IS the perpendicular bisector of the side of $\triangle A'B'C'$ adjacent to $A$.
**Use the known fact.** Perpendicular bisectors of any triangle concur (at the circumcenter). So the altitudes of $\triangle ABC$ concur too.
**Pattern lesson.** "Three things concur" is often proved by reducing to another concurrence that's already known. Reframe the problem; don't slug it out coordinate-by-coordinate.

questions.prompt: Which classical triangle centers can lie OUTSIDE the triangle for some shapes?
choices: Only the orthocenter
Both the circumcenter AND the orthocenter (for obtuse triangles)
All four — centroid, circumcenter, orthocenter, incenter — can be outside
None — all four are always inside
hints: Centroid: average of vertices — must be inside (convex hull). Incenter: bounded by the three sides — also inside.
Circumcenter and orthocenter can both escape outside (in obtuse triangles).
questions.explanation: The centroid is the average of the three vertex positions, so it's always inside the triangle (convex combination). The incenter is bounded by the three angle bisectors and stays inside. But both the circumcenter and the orthocenter migrate outside the triangle when the triangle is obtuse — they're the 'restless' centers.
steps: **Centroid: always inside.** $G = (A+B+C)/3$ is a convex combination of the vertices, so it's inside the triangle.
**Incenter: always inside.** As the meeting of three internal angle bisectors, the incenter is constrained by the triangle's interior.

**Circumcenter and orthocenter: can escape.** In an obtuse triangle, both $O$ and $H$ slip outside. For a right triangle: $O$ sits on the hypotenuse (boundary) and $H$ at the right-angle vertex (boundary). For an acute triangle they're both inside.
**Pattern lesson.** The 'inside-only' centers are defined by averages; the 'sometimes-outside' centers are defined by perpendicular constructions that can extend past the triangle's edges.

questions.prompt: The ALTITUDE of triangle ABC from vertex A is most precisely defined as:
choices: The segment from A to the midpoint of BC
The angle bisector at A
The segment from A perpendicular to (the line containing) BC, ending at the foot of the perpendicular
The perpendicular bisector of BC
hints: Altitude = perpendicular dropped from a vertex.
The foot lies on the OPPOSITE SIDE (or its extension if the triangle is obtuse).
questions.explanation: The altitude from $A$ is the perpendicular segment from $A$ to the LINE through $B$ and $C$ — its endpoint (the 'foot') is on segment $BC$ for acute triangles, ON $BC$'s extension for obtuse triangles. Distinct from the median (vertex to midpoint), the angle bisector (bisects angle $A$), and the perpendicular bisector (bisects $BC$).
steps: **The four 'special segments' from a vertex.** From vertex $A$ you can drop four classical segments to the opposite side: the median (to the midpoint), the altitude (perpendicular to the side), the angle bisector (bisects $\angle A$), and a perpendicular bisector starts at the midpoint not at $A$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="140,30 50,160 230,160" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="140" y1="30" x2="140" y2="160" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.8" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><rect x="134" y="150" width="10" height="10" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.2"/><circle cx="140" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="140" cy="30" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="50" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="230" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="135" y="22" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="36" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="236" y="175" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="148" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">altitude from A</text><text x="148" y="175" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">foot</text></svg>

steps: **Altitude specifically.** Perpendicular line from $A$ down to (the line of) $BC$, terminating at the foot.
**Edge case.** For an obtuse triangle, the foot may land OUTSIDE segment $BC$ — on its extension. The altitude is still well-defined; you just measure along the perpendicular line.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with sides } 13, 14, 15. \\ \text{Find the length of the altitude to the side of length } 14.
hints: Semi-perimeter $s = 21$. Heron: $K = \sqrt{21 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6} = \sqrt{7056} = 84$.
Altitude to side $b$ satisfies $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h_b$. So $h_b = \dfrac{2K}{b}$.
questions.explanation: Use Heron's formula for the area, then $h_b = 2K/b$. Semi-perimeter $s = 21$. $K = \sqrt{21 \cdot 8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6} = \sqrt{7056} = 84$. Altitude to side $14$: $h = 2 \cdot 84 / 14 = 12$.
steps: **Recipe.** Altitude length = $2 \cdot \text{Area} / \text{base}$. We need the area first.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><polygon points="50,160 200,160 95,40" fill="#fef3c7" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="95" y1="40" x2="95" y2="160" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.6" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/><rect x="89" y="150" width="10" height="10" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.2"/><circle cx="50" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="200" cy="160" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><circle cx="95" cy="40" r="3.5" fill="#1e293b"/><text x="36" y="176" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">A</text><text x="206" y="176" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">C</text><text x="82" y="32" font-size="13" font-weight="600" fill="#0f172a">B</text><text x="58" y="108" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">c = 13</text><text x="144" y="108" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">a = 15</text><text x="118" y="176" font-size="11" fill="#0f172a" font-style="italic">b = 14 (base)</text><text x="102" y="100" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626" font-style="italic">h = ?</text></svg>

steps: **Find the area by Heron's formula.** $s = (13+14+15)/2 = 21$. $K = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)} = \sqrt{21 \cdot 6 \cdot 7 \cdot 8} = \sqrt{7056} = 84$. (A famous 'all-integer' triangle.)
**Compute the altitude.** $\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot b \cdot h_b \;\Longrightarrow\; h_b = \dfrac{2 \cdot 84}{14} = 12$.

**Pattern lesson.** Any triangle's altitude to a chosen base = $\dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Area}}{\text{that base}}$. Three different altitudes correspond to three different bases — always tied together by this formula.
